name: Neon
name_origin: From Greek 'neos' (new), reflecting its discovery as a new element separated from liquid air shortly after argon and krypton.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Neon is the fifth most abundant element in the universe but rare on Earth, comprising only ~18 ppm by volume of the atmosphere. Commercial neon is obtained as a byproduct of the cryogenic fractional distillation of liquid air.
states: abundant in stars and gas giants

uses: Neon signs and indicator lamps (characteristic red-orange glow)
Helium-neon (HeNe) gas lasers used in barcode scanners and alignment instruments
Cryogenic refrigerant (40x the refrigerating capacity per volume of liquid helium, 3x of liquid hydrogen)
Buffer gas in deep ultraviolet (excimer) lithography lasers for semiconductor manufacturing
Plasma displays and specialty fluorescent lamps
reactivity_summary: Neon is the second-least reactive element and forms no known stable neutral compounds; only a few transient ions and clathrate-like species have been observed in mass-spectrometry or matrix-isolation experiments. Its complete octet (2s2 2p6) gives it a very high ionization energy and zero electron affinity.
isotopes_summary: Neon has three stable isotopes: 20Ne (~90.5%), 22Ne (~9.3%), and 21Ne (~0.27%). Isotopic ratios in meteorites and lunar samples have been used to study cosmic-ray exposure and the composition of the early solar system.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Neon
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
